card.title: Brodinsky-sur-Boue
card.shortDescription: An interdisciplinary collective exploring contemporary art beyond conventional formats.

gallery.caption: Original theater play MAXINE//BETHANIE, Grange de Dorigny - 2023, Photograph: Kamil Wodzislawski
Image taken at the SON/NET/S exhibition, Bruit rose - 2024
Original theater play ANDROMAQUE_RELOAD\ED, Fri'scènes - 2022, Photograph: Andreas Eggler

page.fullDescription: United by a desire to broaden their respective artistic horizons, the members of BSB seek to combine their skills to enable interdisciplinary and organic contemporary creations. Together, we've carried out several live theater performances (for which I composed and performed the music), art performances, and exhibitions/installations across Suisse romande including at La Grange de Dorigny, Le Bruit Rose, and Friscènes. Over the years, this has added up to dozens of performances in theaters, largely centered on singing and playing music live, but also recording videos and staging.

card.title: Programmatic Artworks
card.shortDescription: Computational processes generating artistic outcomes integrating visuals, sound, and interactivity.

gallery.caption: The Annunciation (2021)
gallery.general_text: A reinterpretation of the biblical scene, The annunciation is a programmatic artwork coded in the python programming language with tools usually used for scientific figures creation. At the core of the Bible’s myth, the annunciation is the moment where the Angel Gabriel announces to Mary that she carries God’s son. It represents the encounter between two worlds: the divine and the human. The artist chose this singular moment out of many others depicted all throughout Christian art history, as a metaphor of the bridge it creates between Science and Art History. Scientific tools were used to represent a classical biblical scene: two charts included in a figure. Mathematic equations applied on statistical distributions were used to create the motion in the image that represents the Angel wings. Moreover, pale colours and gold evoke the washed-out appearance of medieval artworks. Therefore, The annunciation presents itself as part of an artistic tradition, while re-enacting it with the help of contemporary techniques.
gallery.technical_text:
Diptych of images created using python’s matplotlib plotting library. The uniform() function of the random library was used to create the random features of the image such as marker size, or position. The use of statistical distribution (here uniform) with a randomisation of the composition elements properties allows the creation of unique images each time the code is run. For this composition, custom equations were used to represent the wings of Angel Gabriel. These equations allow the randomly and uniformly distributed line markers in this scatterplot to follow the composition movements.
Left wing rotation = 90 * (x + y)
Right wing rotation = -180 * x * y
The code used to produce these images is available on github: https://github.com/Mass23/fig1a_2022/.

gallery.caption: A Living Past (2022)
gallery.general_text: The birth and death of species is at the core of the theory of evolution. However, the importance of previously extinct species in shaping biodiversity by absence is often disregarded. Natural selection is often described as “the survival of the fittest” (Spencer, 1864) within a given species, even though the “extinction of the less fit” is of equal importance by liberating previously occupied niches and shaping the biotic interactions landscape. A living past, aims at interpreting these truncated branches of the tree of life, giving extinct species a regained visual identity, and a meaningful contrast underlining the moment of their extinction. On the verge of Earth’s sixth mass extinction event, this polyptych pays tribute to all extinct lineages that have taken part in an overwhelming proportion to life on our planet, and to all the ones that are yet to disappear, including us humans.
gallery.technical_text:
Polyptych of images created using python’s matplotlib plotting library. The uniform() function of the random library was used to create the random features of the image such as dot size, or position. The use of statistical distribution (here uniform) with a randomisation of the composition elements properties allows the creation of unique images each time the code is run. The use of colour palettes, along with the randomised features of this scatterplot allow to separate the fifth element of the polyptych.
The code used to produce these images is available on github: https://github.com/Mass23/fig1a_2022/.

gallery.caption: ALWAYS (2023)
gallery.general_text: In social sciences, intersectionality serves as a framework aiming to elucidate the intricate interplay among factors such as race, gender, class, and various identity markers, in shaping the intricate patterns of oppression and domination. In this artwork, each element of the quadriptych shows GDP and wealth data overlayed with a line symbolically depicting the reduction in glaciers' area since the 1970s. Through this visual representation, the artwork links one of the most conspicuous effect of climate change with multiple indicators associated with capitalism and disparities. In doing so, it conceptually portrays the intersectionality inherent in the roles of economy, colonialism, and greed, in collectively driving the ongoing climate crisis.
gallery.technical_text: Programmatic images created using data of GDP, glacier surface area and the share of the total net worth held by the top 1%. The graphs were produced using the R programming language and the ggplot2, tidyverse and showtext packages. This work is licensed under the Open Data Commons Open Database License (ODbL), feel free to copy, distribute, transmit and adapt it. More information , the conda environment and the code are freely available on GitHub: GitHub.com/Mass23/ALWAYS.

page.fullDescription: In my programmatic artworks, I use computational processes to generate artistic outcomes. Through code and programs, I create works that integrate visuals, sound, and interactivity, operating at the intersection of technology and aesthetics. I frequently include references to scientific methods and techniques, repurposing a technical medium for artistic expression and challenging the historical division between art and science. My practice also includes glitch art, internet art, sound design, and audiovisual compositions. Projects often combine multiple mediums to achieve outcomes tailored to specific conceptual frameworks. Code functions as a tool to manipulate digital artifacts, model abstract systems, and produce results aligned with the intended goals of each work.

artworks.title: The Annunciation
artworks.description: A reinterpretation of the biblical scene, The annunciation is a programmatic artwork coded in the python programming language with tools usually used for scientific figures creation. At the core of the Bible's myth, the annunciation is the moment where the Angel Gabriel announces to Mary that she carries God's son. It represents the encounter between two worlds: the divine and the human. The artist chose this singular moment out of many others depicted all throughout Christian art history, as a metaphor of the bridge it creates between Science and Art History. Scientific tools were used to represent a classical biblical scene: two charts included in a figure. Mathematic equations applied on statistical distributions were used to create the motion in the image that represents the Angel wings. Moreover, pale colours and gold evoke the washed-out appearance of medieval artworks. card.title: How I Create My Identity
card.shortDescription: An installation exploring identity through the lens of time and space.

gallery.caption: Pixels archive from the installation / exhibition.
gallery.general_text:
I found the needle in the haystack
A thousand billion years ago

A defining moment
A precise occasion
A breach in the universe
A glimpse into the truth

I lost my nightmares in my dreams
A thousand billion times over

I heard god is now the devil
I heard god is now the devil

Everything in the universe aligned to perfection

I gave my body to the fire
A thousand billion years from now

A restoring moment
A bath onto the moon
A breach in the universe
A glimpse into the next one

page.fullDescription: Through the exploration of the artist’s identity, the installation “How-I-create-my-identity” investigates the structure of one’s identity, particularly through the lens of time and space.  Time is symbolically represented by the three visual elements of the installation. The two videos “PAST” and “FUTURE” respectively depict the past (in a diapositive style video) and the future (as projected videos and images). In contrast, the triptych “IDENTITY” composed of motionless programmatic pictures represents the illusion of present. These three visual elements explore the deterministic influence of the past on one’s identity, but also how individuals alter their identity in the future. The music “ULTRASYNCHRONICITY” (https://on.soundcloud.com/pcB1BYEKi2dDWx8npE) further expands on the link between the past, the illusion of present, and the future. Through the deconstruction of past influences, and the reconstruction of a synthetic future identity, the installation “How-I-create-my-identity” invites the viewer to reflect on the formation of their own identity.

exhibitions.title: Structures - PONT 12 (Chavannes-près-Renens), November 2023

card.title: A-glitch-in-the-ethics
card.shortDescription: Glitch-driven exploration of ethical complexities through digital manipulation.

gallery.caption: Where Do We Go From Here (2023)
gallery.general_text: In solidarity with all human beings whose life is or has been at threat. 🇵🇸🕊️
gallery.technical_text: Programmatic artwork, triptych. Screenshot of the Gaza Strip area from OpenStreetMap’s “Humanitarian” map. The image was subsequently processed with python3 using the skimage, matplotlib and opencv2 libraries. Colours match the colours of the palestinian flag, and glitches were created using the glitch_this python command line tool. This work is licensed under the Open Data Commons Open Database License (ODbL), feel free to copy, distribute, transmit and adapt it. The conda environment, code and images are freely available on github: github.com/Mass23/wheredowegofromhere

gallery.caption: Will you call it a genocide if the word is hardcoded in the jpeg file? (2024)
gallery.general_text: In solidarity with all human beings whose life is or has been at threat. 🇵🇸🕊️
gallery.technical_text: Screenshot of the Gaza Strip area from Copernicus’s Sentinel aerial imagery. Difference in pixel colour between 2022 and 2023 was computed and normalised using python3 and the opencv2 library. Subsequently, the word ”GENOCIDE” was hardcoded 666 times randomly into the jpeg format image. Several images were created using different random seeds.

gallery.caption: Drunk Landscape (2025)
gallery.general_text:
Drunk Landscape is a generative digital artwork exploring a cultural landscape as an example of humanity’s far-reaching environmental impact. The work combines glitched composites of Vipera aspis (asp viper) with images of anthropogenic transformation in Lavaux, a UNESCO World Heritage vineyard region in Switzerland. While celebrated for its terraced vineyards, the landscape is shaped by intensive viticulture, pesticide use, and dense infrastructure. Within this altered environment, the artist has followed populations of Vipera aspis for several years, a species now critically endangered on the Swiss plateau. Remarkably, these snakes persist in vineyard walls, embankments, and marginal vegetation, becoming symbols of resilience in a heavily anthropised ecosystem. 
 The title Drunk Landscape refers to both ecological and societal disorientation. The glitch aesthetic reflects a fractured ecosystem, while “drunkenness” also alludes to Switzerland’s alcohol consumption: in 2022, 16.4% of the population drank at harmful levels, with around 1,500 alcohol-related deaths annually. Like the society that celebrates it, the landscape appears harmonious from afar but reveals instability upon closer inspection. The work belongs to the A-glitch-in-the-ethics series, which uses digital manipulation to examine ethical tensions between human activity, the environment, and society.
gallery.technical_text: Drunk Landscape was created using a custom Python script that manipulates photographs through programming. Photographs of Vipera aspis and signs of anthropogenic impact were acquired in the Lavaux region at sites where the species persists despite intensive land use. The images were processed using the NumPy and Pillow libraries. Regions of high contrast in the images were identified, and horizontal stripes were extracted and centered to form the compositional base. The paired images were then blended via pixel-level operations. To produce the glitch aesthetic, 80,000 localized distortions—color perturbations, pixel scrambling, and noise overlays—were algorithmically applied to the image.

page.fullDescription: A Glitch in the Ethics uses digital manipulation and glitch aesthetics to explore the ethical complexities surrounding human actions, both in relation to the natural world and to broader societal issues.

artworks.title: Where Do We Go From Here
Will You Call It a Genocide If the Word Is Hardcoded in the JPEG File?
Drunk Landscape
artworks.description:
Drunk Landscape is a generative digital artwork that examines one cultural landscape as a telling example of humanity's far-reaching impact on the environment. Composed of glitched composites that merge photographs of Vipera aspis (asp viper) with images of anthropogenic impact in the same environment, the work confronts the viewer with the contradictions of Lavaux, a UNESCO World Heritage site in Switzerland. Though widely admired for its vineyards and terraced hills, Lavaux bears the deep imprint of human transformation: the dry meadows that once covered these slopes—biodiversity-rich ecosystems crucial to native fauna—have been systematically replaced by vineyards and infrastructure. Nationwide, more than 95% of these meadows have disappeared since 1900.

The landscape today is shaped by intensive viticulture, which has driven the widespread use of pesticides and led to the concretisation of space through roads, walls, highways, and rail infrastructure. Within this heavily altered terrain, the artist has spent years following populations of Vipera aspis, a species now critically endangered on the Swiss plateau. Remarkably, the snakes persist in these hostile environments—inhabiting vineyard walls, embankments, and marginal patches of vegetation. In this context, the viper is framed as a kind of extremophile—an organism surviving in a deeply anthropised ecosystem where most native habitats have been replaced—a symbol of resilience, adapting to an environment saturated with chemicals and fractured by human infrastructure.

The title Drunk Landscape alludes to this disorientation—visual, ecological, and societal. The glitch aesthetic reflects a fractured ecosystem, and the notion of "drunkenness" also evokes a social reality: as of 2022, 16.4% of the Swiss population consumes alcohol at harmful levels, with approximately 1,500 alcohol-related deaths annually. The landscape, much like the society that celebrates it, appears coherent from a distance but is internally unstable. This work is part of the A-glitch-in-the-ethics series, where digital manipulation and glitch aesthetics are used to explore the ethical complexities surrounding human actions, both in relation to the natural world and to broader societal issues.
artworks.technicalDescription: Drunk Landscape was created using a custom Python script that manipulates photographs through programming. Photographs of Vipera aspis and signs of anthropogenic impact were acquired in the Lavaux region at sites where the species persists despite intensive land use. The images were processed using the NumPy and Pillow libraries. Regions of high contrast in the images were identified, and horizontal stripes were extracted and centered to form the compositional base. The paired images were then blended via pixel-level operations. To produce the glitch aesthetic, 80,000 localized distortions—color perturbations, pixel scrambling, and noise overlays—were algorithmically applied to the image.

card.title: Crumbling the Shoulders of Giants
card.shortDescription: A generative artwork exploring climate change and eroding trust in science.

page.fullDescription:
Crumbling the Shoulders of Giants is a generative digital artwork that explores the tension between the visible impacts of climate change in alpine environments and the increasing public disbelief in scientific evidence. Trust in science is eroding—not only ideologically, but structurally. Research funding is shrinking as political priorities shift toward militarization and short-term crisis management. While the climate emergency accelerates, meaningful action is delayed by denial, disinformation, or distraction.

Through simulated landslides rendered on high-resolution photographs, the work draws parallels between the physical destabilization of mountainous terrain and a broader societal erosion—where foundational systems built on knowledge begin to collapse. Alpine regions in Switzerland are increasingly prone to landslides due to permafrost thaw and intensified weather patterns. These landscapes are becoming more hostile to human activity—trails close, infrastructures break, and safety becomes uncertain. The glitch techniques mirror this transformation, fragmenting the images into visual ruptures. A custom soundscape, echoing the tension of shifting terrain and distant landslides, reinforces the atmosphere of instability.

The title reworks Newton's "standing on the shoulders of giants," implying that the scientific and cultural foundations once trusted are now ignored or destabilized. Part of the Childhood Paradise Reenacted series, the piece also engages with themes of personal memory and collective space, linking the aesthetics of collapse to a broader reflection on how humans—like the ecosystems they inhabit—must now adapt to a reality of environmental unpredictability.

exhibitions.title: Figure1a competition 2025 - Extreme Environments
exhibitions.description: 'Crumbling the Shoulders of Giants' selected by the jury, Exhibition: Hôtel de Ville Lausanne

page.technicalDescription: Crumbling the Shoulders of Giants was developed using custom Python scripts to simulate landslide dynamics in photographic images of alpine landscapes. The process involves algorithmically shifting image blocks to mimic the displacement of soil and rock, creating visual analogs of geological instability. Pixel-level manipulations were implemented using the Pillow and OpenCV2 libraries, including pixel scrambling and noise overlays to simulate physical disturbance. Source images were captured by the artist during hikes in Switzerland between 2020 and 2025. A custom sound layer, composed of synthetic textures, was integrated to support the audiovisual presentation.

card.title: AI MANIFESTO 2026
card.shortDescription: A 15-minute narrative video confronting AI saturation in contemporary creative practice.

page.fullDescription: AI MANIFESTO 2026 is a 15-minute glitch-saturated video manifesto confronting AI's role in contemporary creative practice. Refusing both celebration and rejection, it sits inside the contradictions of authorship, novelty, and the environmental cost of digital practices. Part narration, part self-undermining instruction, the work closes by disclosing its own production tools, treating the question "Is AI art, art?" as a site to inhabit rather than resolve.

exhibitions.title: AI and Forms of Life conference

page.technicalDescription:
AI MANIFESTO 2026 was developed as a programmatic video work, combining custom Python-based processing pipelines with a structured narrative layer. The visual texture of the piece — its glitches, distortions, and temporal ruptures — was generated through frame-level manipulation scripts, extending techniques developed across earlier works in the series. Narration was recorded by the artist and cleaned of room acoustics using Clarity DeReverb, then processed and mixed alongside a composed soundtrack using Sonible SmartEQ4 and SmartLimit.

Voice synthesis was produced using coqui-tts, trained on a sample of the artist's voice, generating synthetic speech from written segments of the narration and blurring the boundary between recorded and generated presence. Upscaling of video frames and source images was performed using ESPCN and EDSR x4 and x3 models, applied selectively to introduce resolution inconsistencies as an intentional aesthetic rather than correcting them. Text generation, scripting assistance, and code development were supported by Claude Sonnet 4.6.
